verdict: Moderate risk of AI displacement, with some tasks being highly automatable but others requiring human judgment and empathy.

reasoning_steps.title: High routine_cognitive tasks
reasoning_steps.body: Task 1: Record patients' medical information and vital signs, Task 2: Administer medications to patients and monitor patients for reactions or side effects, Task 3: Maintain accurate, detailed reports and records, Task 4: Monitor, record, and report symptoms or changes in patients' conditions. These tasks are highly automatable by current LLMs.

reasoning_steps.title: Low creative_judgment
reasoning_steps.body: Tasks 5-8: Provide health care, first aid, immunizations, or assistance in convalescence or rehabilitation; Consult and coordinate with healthcare team members to assess, plan, implement, or evaluate patient care plans; Direct or supervise less-skilled nursing or healthcare personnel; Monitor all aspects of patient care. These tasks require human judgment and empathy.

reasoning_steps.title: Strong regulatory_moat
reasoning_steps.body: Licensure is required for nurses in most states, and the profession has a strong regulatory framework to ensure patient safety.

adjacent_careers.name: Health Educator
adjacent_careers.reasoning: Both nurses and health educators focus on promoting health and wellness, but health educators work in more community-based settings.

adjacent_careers.name: Medical Assistant
adjacent_careers.reasoning: Medical assistants perform some administrative tasks similar to those of nurses, but their role is generally less complex and less autonomous.

recent_developments.headline: New AI-powered chatbot for patient engagement
recent_developments.impact: This new technology may automate some aspects of patient communication, but human nurses will still be necessary to interpret and respond to patients' needs.

recent_developments.headline: FDA clears more AI diagnostic tools each quarter
recent_developments.impact: Radiology, pathology, and dermatology are seeing AI second-readers move into routine clinical workflows.

quotes.quote: AI in medicine should be evaluated like a drug — for safety, for efficacy, and for the population it actually helps.
quotes.speaker: Eric Topol
quotes.title: Cardiologist; Director, Scripps Research Translational Institute

quotes.quote: We should stop training radiologists now. It is just completely obvious that within five years, deep learning is going to do better than radiologists.
quotes.speaker: Geoffrey Hinton
quotes.title: Turing Award laureate; ex-Google
quotes.source: Machine Learning and the Market for Intelligence conference, Toronto

quotes.quote: AI will not replace doctors, but doctors who use AI will replace doctors who don't.
quotes.speaker: Curtis Langlotz
quotes.title: Professor of Radiology and Biomedical Informatics, Stanford
